headline: Nvidia's standalone Vera CPU adds a new pressure point to tight DRAM supply
summary: Nvidia's decision to sell its Vera CPU as a standalone chip (not only bundled in Vera Rubin NVL72 boards) is expected to create fresh demand for low-power DRAM, lifting the LPDDR/SOCAMM2 outlook for Samsung, SK Hynix and Micron into an already supply-constrained memory market.

headline: Samsung set to supply roughly half of Nvidia's 2026 SOCAMM2 modules
summary: Reports indicate Nvidia has asked memory makers for up to ~20 billion GB of SOCAMM2 modules for 2026, with Samsung in line to supply about half (~10 billion GB). SOCAMM2 debuts with the Vera CPU, offering ~50% higher capacity and ~20% better power efficiency than the first generation; SK Hynix and Micron are also ramping competing modules.

headline: GE Vernova emerges as the preferred AI-power trade as quarterly orders top all of last year
summary: Commentary frames GE Vernova as displacing pure-play generators like Vistra in the AI-power trade: GEV's signed multi-year backlog, written guidance raises and visible margin expansion contrast with generators whose upside leans on not-yet-signed hyperscaler PPAs. Vertiv organic orders reportedly grew ~81%, underscoring equipment-maker order strength.

headline: Power, not chips, remains the gating factor for AI datacenter buildouts
summary: Industry trackers continue to flag that a large share of announced AI datacenter projects face delays tied to power-infrastructure bottlenecks rather than chip supply, keeping grid equipment, turbines and baseload generation (including nuclear) central to the buildout. Europe's datacenter association similarly cites energy availability as the main growth constraint.
